title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Likelihood Distribution

# Set parameters
successes <- 20
trials <- 50

# Generate a sequence of theta values
theta_values <- seq(0.01, 0.99, by = 0.001)

likelihood_function <- function(theta, successes, trials) {
  return (theta^successes * (1 - theta)^(trials - successes))
}

# Calculate the likelihood for each theta
likelihood_values <- sapply(theta_values, likelihood_function, successes = successes, trials = trials)

# Plot the likelihood function
plot(theta_values, likelihood_values, type = "l", col = "blue", 
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = "Likelihood", 
     main = "Likelihood Function")

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Posterior Distribution

# Posterior distribution of theta

curve(dbeta(x, shape1 = 22, shape2 = 38), 
      from = 0, to = 1, 
      col = "darkblue", 
      lwd = 2, 
      ylab = "Density", 
      xlab = expression( ~ theta),
      main = expression("Posterior beliefs of " ~ theta)) 







1 - pbeta(0.2, shape1 = 22, shape2 = 38)

[1] 0.9984086

pbeta(0.555, shape1 = 22, shape2 = 38) - pbeta(0.25, shape1 = 22, shape2 = 38)

[1] 0.9738378


So, we know that we had a prior distribution for \(\theta\) that had a mean of 0.20. Then we observed 20/50 successes and 30/50 failures, meaning the observed proportion of people with two minor alleles was 0.40. Then we combined these two pieces to obtain a beta distribution with parameters (22, 38). The expected value of this distribution, or in other words our updated beliefs about the proportion of people who have two minor alleles is 0.3667. Furthermore there is 99% chance that \(\theta\) is greater than 0.2 and a 97% chance that the value of \(\theta\) is between 0.25 and 0.55. So we think we can be fairly confident that the value of \(\theta\) is above the prior value.

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
GWAS/Regression using a Bayesian framework
To restate the goal of this project, we want to implement a Bayesian framework into a genome wide association study.   Let’s first discuss how a genome wide association study works using a frequentist approach. Simply put the idea of a genome wide association studies is to try and find genetic variants (single nucleotide polymorphisms or SNPs) that are associated with a specific trait. We often have thousands or even hundreds of thousands of SNPs that we want to check to see if they are associated with the trait of interest. To do this we run a marginal regression models which means we create a regression model for each SNP as the predictor and the trait of interest as the outcome variable and collect a point estimate and p-value from the model. Once we have collected a point estimate and p-value for each SNP, we order them from lowest to highest(most significant to least significant) p-value to see if there are any SNPs that have a statistically significant effect on the outcome variable. Usually we look for p-values below \(5 \times 10^{-8}\). You interpret these p-values as the probability of observing this point estimate if the null hypothesis (no relationship between SNP and trait) is true. This isn’t a very intuitive result and one of the main reasons we shift to explore a Bayesian approach.   So to take a Bayesian approach and implement it into a genome wide association study, we need to learn how Bayesian regression works, and then we just implement it for each SNP. A simple linear regression model has the following form:
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X\]
Meaning we have three unknown parameters that we are trying to estimate. \(\beta_0\), our intercept. \(\beta_1\), our slope. \(\sigma^2\), the variance of our y values. This means we first have to set a prior for each one of those parameters. \[\beta_0 \sim \; ???\] This represents our prior beliefs of our intercept coefficient, meaning what is the expected value or values of our outcome variable if the predictor variable equals zero.   \[\beta_1 \sim \; ???\] This represents our prior beliefs of our slope coefficient, meaning what do we expect the effect size of the predictor variable to be or how much will the outcome variable change on average if the predictor increases by one.   \[\sigma^2 \sim \; ???\] Lastly, the prior distribution of the variance of our outcome variable.   Then we need to create a likelihood function, which represents what values of \(\beta_0\), \(\beta_1\), and \(\sigma^2\) are most plausible given our observed data. This function tends to have the following form, and will usually be distributed normally. \[ Y \; | \; \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2 \sim  \; N(\beta_0 + \beta_1X, \sigma^2)\]   Then we put all of these together to derive a posterior distribution for our parameters of interest. \[ \pi(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2 \; | \; Y)\propto f(Y \; | \; \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2) \pi(\beta_0)\pi(\beta_1)\pi(\sigma^2)\] This distribution contains our updated beliefs about our parameters of interest. One very important thing to note is that this posterior probability distribution is very difficult (and maybe impossible) to derive analytically or by hand. The reason being because the normalizing constant is found by integrating the product of the likelihood and prior over all possible values of the parameters. So the normalizing constant of the above posterior distribution follows the following form shown in the denominator of the fraction.  \[\pi(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2 \; | \; Y) =  \frac{f(Y \; | \; \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2) \pi(\beta_0)\pi(\beta_1)\pi(\sigma^2)} {\int\int\int  f(Y \; | \; \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2)\;\pi(\beta_0)\pi(\beta_1)\pi(\sigma^2)  d\beta_0\beta_1d\sigma^2}\]  Instead of solving this, we are instead going to simulate our posterior distribution and then get separate distributions for each parameters. This will be done using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). We are not experts on this tool nor is it the focus of our project but we want to discuss it briefly. MCMC combines two ideas: Markov chains and Monte Carlo. Monte Carlo which is a statistical tool for repeated sampling. Markov chains is a process of describing a sequence of possible events in which the probability of the next event only depends on the state of the current event. So when we combine these two tools we get a way to estimate very complex probability distributions, like the posterior of a Bayesian regression.

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Creating the data
 We are creating our own data for the purpose of this example. In this case we are looking at 100 peoples heights, and then 4 SNPs in each of their genomes. At each SNP everyone has a 64% chance of 0 minor alleles, a 32% chance of 1 minor allele, and a 4% chance of 2 minor alleles. Then each persons height is randomly selected from a normal distribution. It is also important to note that everyones height also depends on the number of minor alleles that they have at SNP4 and SNP2.

set.seed(494)

# Generating data
n <- 100

# generate data
SNP1 <- sample(0:2, size = n, replace = T, prob = c(.64, .32, .04))
SNP2 <- sample(0:2, size = n, replace = T, prob = c(.64, .32, .04))
SNP3 <- sample(0:2, size = n, replace = T, prob = c(.64, .32, .04))
SNP4 <- sample(0:2, size = n, replace = T, prob = c(.64, .32, .04))
height <- rnorm(n, mean = 70, sd = 5) + 5*SNP4 + 0*SNP3 + 2*SNP2 + 0*SNP1 

data <- as.data.frame(cbind(height, SNP1, SNP2, SNP3, SNP4))

data %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=height))+
  geom_density()







data %>% head(3)

    height SNP1 SNP2 SNP3 SNP4
1 66.06921    0    1    0    0
2 78.45188    0    0    0    1
3 72.58425    0    0    0    0

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Our model
So now we take the Bayesian regression framework and implement into a genome wide association study. We start with the following regression model, and remember because we are doing a GWAS and using a marginal regression this type of model will be fit with each SNP in our data set. In this case our intercept is noted as \(\theta\), our slope coefficient is \(SNP_{Effect}\), and the variance of the heights (which is seen in the likelihood function in the next section) is noted as \(\epsilon\).
\[Height = \theta + SNP_{Effect}\;x\]

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Likelihood function
Then we must set a likelihood function. In this case we will have a normally distributed likelihood function because we know that heights are a normally distributed random variable with a mean and some variance. What does this likelihood function means in words? It represents what values of \(\theta\), \(SNP_{Effect}\), and \(\epsilon\) make our observed data, Height(Y/outcome) and SNPs(X/predictor), most plausible. \[ Height \; | \; \theta, \; SNP_{Effect}, \; \epsilon \sim \; N(\theta + SNP_{Effect}x, \; \epsilon)\]

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Prior distributions
First, the prior for \(\theta\). We set the prior distribution of \(\theta\) as a normal distribution with a mean of 70 and a variance of 5. What this means is that we expect peoples heights to be centered around 70 inches if they have zero minor alleles or if a SNP has no effect, but this could also vary slightly. Then, the prior for \(SNP_{Effect}\). This represents our prior beliefs about what we think the increase in height will be if the number of minor alleles increases by one or what we think the effect of a SNP will be on the outcome variable height. It is centered at zero because we tend to think that the majority (~99% or more) of SNPs do not have an effect on height, but once again this could also vary a little bit. Finally, the prior for \(\epsilon\). This is representative of our beliefs about the variance of our heights and could represent other factors that influence height beyond genetic information. \[\theta \sim \; N(70,5) , \; SNP_{Effect} \sim \; N(0,1)  , \; \epsilon  \sim \; Gamma(1,0.2)\]
All of these priors are shown below. 

plot_normal(mean = 70, sd = 5) + 
  labs(x = "Theta", y = "pdf")







plot_normal(mean = 0, sd = 1) + 
  labs(x = "SNP Effect", y = "pdf")







plot_gamma(shape = 1, rate = 0.2) + 
  labs(x = "Epsilon", y = "pdf")

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Posterior distribution
Now, our posterior distribution which we mentioned will actually get simulated, but it will have the form shown below.
\[ \pi(\theta, \; SNP_{Effect}, \; \epsilon  \; | \; Height) \; \propto \; f(Height \; | \; \theta, \; SNP_{Effect}, \; \epsilon) \; \pi(\theta) \; \pi(SNP_{Effect}) \; \pi(\epsilon)\]  This means that our results are a joint probability function of \(\theta\), \(SNP_{Effect}\), \(\epsilon\) given our observed data.

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Simulating the posterior distributions
We mentioned earlier that we must simulate our posterior distribution. Here is the code for how we will do it, and then we will talk about the results below. Remember, we simulated all of this data so we know the truth about each SNP and it’s effect on height. What we want to find out is if this is an effective way to analyze the relationship between SNPs and height. 
Here we are creating a function to run a MCMC simulation for height as a function a one SNP. As you can see it requires you to specify the distribution for all of your priors, which we have done.

# Function to run a MCMC simulation for each variable
pred_posterior <- function(SNP, height, data) {
  gene_mod <- stan_glm(height ~ SNP, data = data,
                       family = gaussian,
                       prior_intercept = normal(70, 5),
                       prior = normal(0, 1), 
                       prior_aux = exponential(0.2),
                       chains = 4, iter = 5000*2, seed = 84735, 
                       refresh = 0,        
                       show_messages = FALSE)
  print(posterior_interval(gene_mod, prob = 0.95))
  mcmc_dens_overlay(gene_mod)
}

 Starting first with SNP1. As a reminder, when we simulated the data SNP1 does not have an effect on height.

pred_posterior(data$SNP1, data$height, data)

                 2.5%     97.5%
(Intercept) 72.490335 75.113351
SNP         -1.768202  1.132637
sigma        5.423766  7.163915









 If we take a look at the results we see it matches our expectation almost perfectly. The posterior distribution for \(SNP_{Effect}\) (located in the middle of the three graphs) is centered right at zero which means that the expected value of the increase in height if the number of minor alleles at SNP1 increases by one unit is zero. Then if we look over to the posterior distribution of the intercept(\(\theta\)) we see that it has changed a bit from our prior distribution. Recall that our prior distribution for \(\theta\) had an expected value of 70, and now we see that \(\theta\) has an expected value of about 74, meaning if someone has zero minor alleles at SNP 1 we would expect them to have a height of about 74 inches. This means that the data that we observed suggests that the expected value for the intercept is higher than we originally thought. This same conclusion is relevant for the next three SNPs. Lastly, the posterior distribution for the variance/sigma(or \(\epsilon\) in our case) is now a little bit higher than the prior belief for \(\epsilon\) suggesting that the heights we observe have more variance than we originally thought. This same conclusion is relevant for the next three SNPs.   Now looking at SNP2. As a reminder, when we simulated the data SNP2 had a small effect on height.

pred_posterior(data$SNP2, data$height, data)

                  2.5%     97.5%
(Intercept) 71.9558738 74.572011
SNP         -0.3147939  2.593274
sigma        5.3326069  7.056943









 Starting first with the posterior for \(SNP_{Effect}\). We see now that the expected value or center of the distribution is right at about 1. This means that for each additional minor allele at SNP2 we would expect that someones height goes up by an average of 1 inch. Remember that when we simulated the data, the actual value for this slope coefficient was 2. What we mean by that is that for each minor allele someone had at SNP2, 2 inches was added on to their total height. The reason the posterior distribution is centered at 1 instead of 2 is because the Bayesian framework is also taking into account our prior beliefs about this slope parameter, which was centered at 0.   Now looking at SNP3. As a reminder, when we simulated the data SNP3 does not have an effect on height.

pred_posterior(data$SNP3, data$height, data)

                 2.5%    97.5%
(Intercept) 72.409337 75.04543
SNP         -1.553144  1.37820
sigma        5.438955  7.19349









 The results we see from SNP 3 are almost identical to the ones we observed from SNP1 because in both cases when we created the data peoples height were not dependent on the number of SNPs someone has at these SNPs.   Now looking at SNP4. As a reminder, when we simulated the data SNP4 had a large effect on height.

pred_posterior(data$SNP4, data$height, data)

                2.5%     97.5%
(Intercept) 71.44541 73.972015
SNP          1.03678  3.801791
sigma        4.95970  6.616405









 Last but not least, SNP4. The posterior distribution for \(SNP_{Effect}\) in this case is most different from the prior distribution. This is what we expect to see given what we know about the data. The expected value of this posterior distribution is about 2.5. This is fairly conservative given the truth that for each additional minor allele at SNP4 someones height will actually increase by 5 inches.

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Drawbacks
There are three main drawbacks from this approach that we have used: computational cost, intellectual cost, and subjectivity.  The computational cost of this approach comes solely from the need to simulate our posterior distributions for \(\theta\), \(SNP_{Effect}\), and \(\epsilon\). In this approach we used the RStan package which is used for defining and analyzing Bayesian models in R. In our case we are using the RStan package for Bayesian inference and more specifically we are using Markov Chain Monte Carlo to estimate our posterior models. This is very computationally expensive because of the amount of iteration that is used. In our very simple understanding of MCMC, each step in the chain involves taking a new sample and evaluating our likelihood function and the three prior distributions. As the data grows the amount of computing power needed to perform this grows as well. In our case we only used 4 SNPs and 100 people, but most genome wide association studies contain thousands of people and more importantly about a million SNPs. So if these methods were used in practice it would be extremely computationally expensive.  Now to quickly touch on the intellectual cost of using this approach. Recall that the results for each regression model (height ~ SNP) that was fit was three distributions, one for each parameter in the model. In a normal GWAS about 1 million SNPs are used. Imagine we had to look at these results for one million SNPs and had to find the ones with the largest change from the prior distributions, we would be looking for a while. One way we could avoid having to do that is finding a way to pull out the mean or expected value from each of the three distributions each time we fit a model (height ~ SNP) and saving them to a data frame. That way we could easily arrange the models/SNP from highest expected effect on height to lowest expected effect on height.  Now, as with any Bayesian approach there is a subjectivity aspect to this analysis. Conclusions from a Bayesian analysis are drawn based on a likelihood function and a prior distribution. The issue arises from our prior distribution which contributes our prior beliefs to the posterior distribution and depends only on the researchers prior beliefs about the association in question, meaning it is influenced by someones interpretations, opinions, or experiences. One way we can reduce the subjectivity of our outcomes is by using non-informative (or weakly informative) priors. These are prior distributions that do not impact the posterior distribution a lot and let the observed data speak for itself.

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
Next steps
There are a couple things we would like to complete in the future: sensitivity analysis and different ways to interpret results.  Starting first with some sensitivity analysis. What we mean by this is: what happens to our posterior distributions if we changed our prior distributions? For example, if our prior for our slope parameter was \(SNP_{Effect} \sim N(0,5)\) instead of \(SNP_{Effect} \sim N(0,1)\), how different would our results be? Would they reflect the truth more closely?  Then we would like to find ways to draw out different conclusions from the RStan/MCMC output. Meaning we can find ways to get credible intervals for each parameter for each marginal regression model. Those would be very helpful because they can be interpreted very easily: there is a \(\_\_\%\) chance that \(SNP_{Effect}\) (Slope) lies between a and b. We can also calculate probabilities that each parameter is above/below a certain value: there is a \(\_\_\%\) chance that \(SNP_{Effect}\) is greater than c. These types of conclusions can help us draw more concrete conclusions about the plausibility that each parameter has a certain value.

title: Implementing Bayesian Statistical Techniques in Genome Wide Association Studies
text:
End
In this project we looked into how Bayesian statistical methods can be implemented into genome wide association studies(GWAS). As we know, GWAS is the process of creating a simple linear regression model for each SNP along the genome (predictor variable) with our trait of interest (outcome variable). We began by introducing the structure of Bayesian statistics, then explained how that structure can be used in a regression setting. Once we understood the methods that were going to be used, we created some data and put them to the test.

theme_gppr <- function(){ 
    font <- "Georgia"   #assign font family up front
    
    theme_minimal() %+replace%    #replace elements we want to change
    
    theme(

      panel.grid.major = element_blank(),    #strip major gridlines
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),    #strip minor gridlines
      axis.ticks = element_blank(),          #strip axis ticks
      
      #since theme_minimal() already strips axis lines, 
      #we don't need to do that again
      
      #text elements
      plot.title = element_text(             #title
                   family = font,            #set font family
                   size = 15,                #set font size
                   face = 'bold',            #bold typeface
                   hjust = 0,                #left align
                   vjust = 2),               #raise slightly

    )
}

title: Final_Phase2
text:
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggmap)
library(broom)


Research Question

How does calorie content differ between McDonalds, Wendy’s, and Burger King, comparing the same food category (i.e. Sandwiches, Salads, Burgers)? In other words, how does fast food restaurant effect the relationship between food category and calorie content of a fast food menu item?


menu_stat <- read_csv("menu_items.csv")

Warning: One or more parsing issues, call `problems()` on your data frame for details,
e.g.:
  dat <- vroom(...)
  problems(dat)




Data Cleaning

#This cleans the data so that it gets rid of all of the menus items that have NA's for nutritional facts. 
menu_stat2 <- menu_stat %>% 
  rename(category = Food_Category) %>% 
  select(restaurant, Item_Name, category, Calories, Total_Fat, Carbohydrates, Protein, Sugar) %>% 
  filter(!category == "Toppings & Ingredients") %>% 
  filter(!is.na(Calories), !is.na(Total_Fat), !is.na(Carbohydrates), !is.na(Protein)) 



Looking at the data

#Second data set idea
final_menu_stat2 <- menu_stat2 %>% 
  filter(restaurant %in% c("McDonald's", "Burger King", "Wendy's")) %>% 
  filter(category %in% c("Burgers", "Fried Potatoes"))

#Burgers are the most common fast food item in America and people often get fries with those burgers. 



Regression Model
\[ E[ Calories |  category * restaurant] = \beta_0 + \beta_1categoryFriedPotatoes + \beta_2RestaurantMcDonald's + \beta_3RestaurantWendy's \\
+ \beta_4categoryFriedPotatoes:RestaurantMcDonald's + \beta_8categoryFriedPotatoes:RestaurantWendy's \]


Fitting the linear regression model

We chose to use a linear regression model with an interaction between the category of the food and the restaurant to predict calorie content. We chose this model because we are trying to examine how calorie content differs among different food categories at fast food restaurants, and we believe that food category might effect calorie content differently based on the restaurant that the food item comes from, and for that reason we chose a linear regression model with an interaction.


Outcome variable = Calories(Quantitative)


Predictor of interest = Food Category(Categorical)


Precision variables: fat, protein, carbohydrates


Effect modifier: restaurant


Explanatory variables that we will use in our model: the interaction between category and restaurant.


final_model <- final_menu_stat2 %>% 
  with(lm(Calories ~ category*restaurant))

# Next we want to know how do find the difference in burger king fries to mcdonalds fries? WE NEED TO REORDER THE VARIABLES?
final_menu_stat2 %>% 
  mutate(category = relevel(droplevels(factor(category)), ref = "Fried Potatoes")) %>% 
  with(lm(Calories ~ category*restaurant))


Call:
lm(formula = Calories ~ category * restaurant)

Coefficients:
                         (Intercept)                       categoryBurgers  
                              385.00                                166.27  
                restaurantMcDonald's                     restaurantWendy's  
                             -117.00                                 12.14  
categoryBurgers:restaurantMcDonald's     categoryBurgers:restaurantWendy's  
                              144.77                                -26.75  

final_menu_stat2 %>% 
  filter(restaurant == "Wendy's", category == "Fried Potatoes") %>% 
  summarise(mean(Calories))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  `mean(Calories)`
             <dbl>
1             397.

final_menu_stat2 %>% 
  filter(restaurant == "Wendy's", category == "Burgers") %>% 
  summarise(mean(Calories))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  `mean(Calories)`
             <dbl>
1             537.




Coefficients and more from the model

tidy(lm(Calories ~ category*restaurant, data = final_menu_stat2))

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  term                                     estimate std.error statistic  p.value
  <chr>                                       <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1 (Intercept)                                 551.       34.6    15.9   1.95e-32
2 categoryFried Potatoes                     -166.       72.9    -2.28  2.42e- 2
3 restaurantMcDonald's                         27.8      65.8     0.422 6.74e- 1
4 restaurantWendy's                           -14.6      65.8    -0.222 8.25e- 1
5 categoryFried Potatoes:restaurantMcDona…   -145.      123.     -1.18  2.40e- 1
6 categoryFried Potatoes:restaurantWendy's     26.7     115.      0.233 8.16e- 1




Creating Confidence Intervals

# Code for confidence intervals
final_model %>% 
  confint()

                                                2.5 %    97.5 %
(Intercept)                                  482.8012 619.74424
categoryFried Potatoes                      -310.5105 -22.03492
restaurantMcDonald's                        -102.4839 158.03370
restaurantWendy's                           -144.8649 115.65274
categoryFried Potatoes:restaurantMcDonald's -387.4052  97.85543
categoryFried Potatoes:restaurantWendy's    -200.1917 253.68958




Relevant Hypothesis test’s

Ho: restaurantMcDonald’s = 0 –> There is no difference in the expected calories of a McDonald’s Burger to a Burger King burger.


Ha: restaurantMcDonald’s != 0 –> There is a difference in the expected calories of a McDonald’s Burger to a Burger King burger.


Ho: restaurantWendy’s = 0 –> There is no difference in the expected calories of a Wendy’s Burger to a Burger King burger.


Ha: restaurantWendy’s != 0 –> There is a difference in the expected calories of a Wendy’s Burger to a Burger King burger.



What do we learn from this model?

When we look at this model the first thing you notice is the intercept coefficient. This coefficient estimates that the calorie content of a Burger King burger is on average 551.27 calories. The you can see from the ‘restaurantMcDonald’s’ coefficient that the estimated calorie increase in a McDonald’s burger from a Burger King burger is 27.7 calories. Additionally the interval from -102.48 to 158.03 gives us a range of plausible values for the true difference in calorie content of a McDonald’s burger and a Burger King burger, and we are 95% confident that the true population value for this coefficient will fall in this interval. Next, you can see from the ‘restaurantWendy’s’ coefficient that the estimated calorie decrease in a Wendy’s burger from a Burger King burger is 14.6 calories. Furthermore, the interval from -102.48 to 158.03 gives us a range of possible values for the true difference in calorie content of Wendy’s burger and a Burger King burger, and we are 95% confident that the true population parameter will fall in that interval. In this model you can see the estimated difference in the calorie content when comparing a burger to fried potatoes among the three restaurant. Overall, from this model we learn that McDonald’s burger tend to be the “least healthy” in terms of calorie content, followed by Burger King and then Wendy’s. We could also find the same thing out about fried potatoes once we learn to reorder the variables and get an intercept for fries instead of burgers.



Hypothesis test decisions

(restaurantMcDonald’s: p-value = 0.6738501) This p-value means that there is about a 67 percent chance of seeing this large of an estimated difference between the calorie content of a McDonald’s burger and the calorie content of a Burger King burger if the null hypothesis is true. With a p-value of 0.67385019(for the ’restaurantMcDonald’s coefficient) there is not statistically significant evidence to suggest that there is a difference between the calorie content of a McDonald’s burger and a Burger King burger. Also, the confidence interval of -102.4839 to 158.03370 does contain the null value of 0. Because of both of these things, we once again fail to reject the null and cannot conclude that there is a difference in the expected calories of a McDonald’s Burger to a Burger King burger.


(restaurantWendy’s: p-value = 0.8247979)This p-value means that there is about a 82 percent chance of seeing this large of an estimated difference between the calorie content of a Wendy’s burger and the calorie content of a Burger King burger if the null value is true. With a p-value of 0.8247979 there is not statistically significant evidence to suggest that there is a difference between the calorie content of a Wendy’s burger and a Burger King burger, and we fail to reject the null hypothesis. Also, the confidence interval of -144.8649 to 115.65274 does contain the null value of 0. Because of both of these things, we once again fail to reject the null and cannot conclude that there is a difference in the expected calories of a Wendy’s Burger to a Burger King burger.



Limitations

During our research and R code, we began to realize that there would be a couple limitations that will take place in our study. For instance, we were forced to exclude some fast food restaurants in our survey. The main reason we did this is because our data set contained over 96 different fast food restaurants, and if we included every single one of those(while using a regression model that examined the effect that restaurant has on calorie content) that would have made our linear model quite long and hard to work with. Another reason we chose to only work with McDonald’s, Burger King and Wendy’s is because we wanted to have nationwide chains (sorry Culvers) that were extremely popular and have locations all over the country. Additionally, we had to filter out a decent amount of menu items because we only chose to work with menu items that fell under the categories of salads, burgers, sandwiches, and fried Potatoes. The four categories that we chose for our model equation were items that were consistent among the restaurants that we chose to work with, they are looked at as some of the more common fast food menu items, they are all relatively equal in volume so the differences in calories among them wont strictly be due to the size of the foods, and lastly they are all considered entrees. For instance, we know that beverages have calories but it is very unfair to compare the calorie content of a drink to the calorie content of a burger or sandwhich.

title: Liberal Arts Education at Macalester
text:
How do students choose to take classes in a breadth of areas to fulfill both general requirements and major specific ones? The data set we are examining shows data from Macalester students who graduated from 2013-2018 regarding information like graduation year, sex, major(s), minor(s), number of classes taken in departments/divisions etc. Because the data provides a holistic view on a student’s education, we want to examine how students of any major interact with classes. How does the number of majors change the number of classes in each division? How does major affect the number of unique departments taken? How does the division of majors affect the number of classes taken within each division? We hope that by approaching the topic from a few different perspectives such as major count, unique departments, and department variety, we can learn a little bit about what it means to be a student, regardless of what category one may fall into.



This visualization shows the difference in the types of classes that students take depending on how many majors they have. Selecting one, two, or three majors will subsect the data and show how many classes in each division students with the selected number of majors took. Interestingly enough, there is little difference between the number of classes a student takes with one or two majors. But you can see that when you look at the students with three majors, the Natural Science and Mathematics bar is clearly taller than the rest of the divisions. The reason for this may be because it’s easier to have multiple majors within the Natural Science and Math division because there is some overlap between the requirements for majors within that division. When looking at students with one major, Natural Science and Math, Humanities, and Social Sciences are relatively equal which shows that majors in those divisions are equally popular among students with one major. Examining the graph that shows that of students with two majors, the Humanities majors take slightly more classes than the Natural Science and Math majors. The reason for this may be because those majors often pair well with majors in other divisions. For example, students may choose to major in a language and pair that with a major in the Interdisciplinary division. There also might not be enough data to draw clear conclusions; out of the approximately 2,500 students looked at, there are about 1800 students who had one major and 650 who had two. This number drops significantly when looking at students who had three majors (19).
 



How do students take courses in and out of their major? Do some majors attract students that take many courses outside of their major? One way to explore how “diverse” Mac students are in terms of their course selection is by looking at how many total departments there are in which a student took a course. This visualization shows how many unique departments each major has taken, with the significant caveat that this filters out all students with more than one major. Doing so results in a different amount of students in each department, but is worth doing because including students with multiple majors would skew certain majors to further extremes. For example, as seen by the visualization of “Number of classes in a division by major” we see that science and math majors are more likely to have multiple majors, and those majors are often within the same division. If we included double and triple majors in this data set, science and math majors could appear significantly less diverse because those students with multiple majors are taking many classes in few departments. Between 2013 and 2018, the average number of unique department prefixes a student took over their time at Macalester was about 12 (11.88 precisely). Some notable departments with low amounts of “department diversity” are Russian Studies, Music, and Women, Gender and Sexuality Studies, and some notable departments with high amounts are American Studies, Sociology, and Latin American Studies. While Interdisciplinary and Social Sciences are the two divisions with the highest average diversity, and Humanities and Natural Science and Math are the two with the least, the divisions are still relatively similar to each other. In fact, all the divisions have between 12-13 majors, with the exception of Fine Arts, which only has three majors. One would expect, based on stereotypes, that STEM majors, for example, might be the least flexible. 
This visualization, however, shows that while there is definitely a discrepancy between majors, it is not especially large; the range between Russian Studies, the minimum, and Latin American Studies, the maximum is only 4.25. If we separate based on division, we get a similar picture, and find that the average number of departments taken for Humanities is the same as Math, around 12.1!  The most important conclusion of this data is that Macalester does a good job of encouraging students to take a wide variety of classes. All divisions have a similar range of unique departments (about three), and when the visualization is expanded to show all majors, the range grows to only 4.25.



Our last visualization shows the average number of classes taken in each division based on the division of the major. It’s important to note that students who had more than one major were counted twice; for example, a Math and Biology double major would have been considered two “people” and course count was considered twice. This is fine to do because if you are majoring in biology and math your class distribution should be shown as a biology major and a math major separately, since that reflects your course choice/interests as a student.
The first thing we notice is that students with majors within the Natural Science and Mathematics division tend to be the most focused on their own division; the discrepancy between Science/Math classes they take and classes they take in other divisions is the greatest. On average, the number of classes that a Natural Science and Math division major would take in that division is 20. On the other hand, the same students would take on average 16 classes outside of the Natural Science and Math division. This makes sense because students majoring in Natural Science and Math departments will often have to take extra classes outside of their majors department. So for example, a physics major would have to take many physics classes, but then also take some math classes and possibly other science classes. All of these classes would be counted as a Natural Science and Math class, therefore the count of Natural Science and Math classes for a physics major(and other math and science majors) would be unusually high.
  The division that had the most equal spread of classes among the five division were the Interdisciplinary majors, which makes sense because Interdisciplinary inherently means relating to more than one branch of knowledge. Those that are learning about multiple subjects would essentially have to take classes in multiple divisions to fulfill the requirements. Another division of majors that had a more balanced variety in the average number of classes taken among divisions is the Social Science majors. To visualize the statements a little more, on average someone majoring in an Interdisciplinary major will take about 12 classes in the Interdisciplinary division, and take about 23 classes outside of the Interdisciplinary division. Similarly, students majoring in Social Sciences will on average take about 14 classes in the Social Science division, and take 20 classes outside of the Social Sciences division. So students in these divisions tend to take more classes outside of their majors division than classes in the division of their major, and you don’t see a huge change in the number of classes taken in their majors division, and classes taken outside of their majors division.
 
From this visualization we learned that the division of your major directly affects the number of classes that you take in each of the five divisions.



To answer the question of what the Macalester Liberal Arts experience looks like, we used a data set describing information about Macalester Students from 2013-2018, and created three different interactive visualizations that explore different aspects of student course choice. Our first visualization explored the relationship between number of majors and classes in each division. In it, we see that single-major students tend to take more classes in the Natural Science and Mathematics, Non-divisional and Social Science divisions, revealing which are the most popular. Students with 3 majors tend to take more classes in Natural Science and Math, showing that people with three majors are more likely majoring in a math or science department. In our second visualization, showing the relationships between major and amount of unique departments taken, we learn that Macalester does a good job of encouraging a variety of class choices, and regardless of major, students take classes in between 10-14 departments, with the exact average being 11.88. Our final visualization shows the relationship between the number of classes taken in each division based on major. This visualization shows that students in the Natural Science and Math division tend to stay within their division more than other divisions. Interdisciplinary, Social Science or Humanities have a more even spread between classes inside and outside of their division.

title: Modeling Trade VS Natural Disasters
text:
Loading the data

# Necessary packages
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(GGally)
library(broom) 

# Loading in the dataset
natural_disaster <- read_csv("nat_disaster_data.csv")
country_trade_data <- read_csv("country_trade_data.csv")



Data wrangling/cleaning

# Creating a data set for each countries number of natural disasters per year over the last 23 years. 
nat_dis_per_year_per_country <- natural_disaster %>% 
  group_by(Country, `Start Year`) %>% 
  summarise(num_of_nat_dis = n()) %>% 
  mutate("Start Year" = as.character(`Start Year`))

# Obtain a data set for each countries trade percentage of GDP over the last 23 years. 
trade_per_year_per_country <- country_trade_data %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = c("1999","2000","2001","2002","2003","2004","2005","2006","2007","2008","2009","2010","2011","2012","2013","2014","2015","2016","2017","2018","2019","2020","2021","2022"), names_to = "Year", values_to = "Trade share of GDP") %>% 
  select(`Country Name`, Year, `Trade share of GDP`)

# We now have a data set that contains a line for each country and year combination, and then along with that is the countries trade share of GDP and number of natural disasters in that year. 
model_data <- trade_per_year_per_country %>% 
  left_join(nat_dis_per_year_per_country, by = c("Country Name" = "Country", "Year" = "Start Year")) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(num_of_nat_dis))



Creating a model

model <- model_data %>%
  with(lm(`Trade share of GDP` ~ num_of_nat_dis))

model %>% 
  tidy()

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  term           estimate std.error statistic  p.value
  <chr>             <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1 (Intercept)       81.8      1.11       73.9 0       
2 num_of_nat_dis    -2.09     0.187     -11.2 3.41e-28

model %>% 
  tidy() %>% 
  summarize(lb = estimate - 2*std.error, ub = estimate + 2*std.error)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
     lb    ub
  <dbl> <dbl>
1 79.6  84.0 
2 -2.47 -1.72




Takeaways from the model


Confidence interval for the slope does not contain zero and all the values in the interval are negative which means we can be sure that there is a negative relationship between natural disasters and the trade share of GDP.
The intercept tells us that if a country faces no natural disasters then on average their trade share of GDP will be about 81.77 %.
The estimate for the slope tells us that on average for every one additional natural disaster that occurs in a country their trade share of GDP will drop by an average of 2.092 %.
P-value for both the intercept and the slope are extremely low meaning that these estimates are statistically significant.
